answers.intake_q1: When did your back pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly your back pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: Any recent trauma or injury to your back or ribs?
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or unexplained weight loss since the pain started?
answers.intake_q5: Can you tell me if the pain changes with breathing, coughing, or movement?
answers.intake_q6: Have you noticed any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your legs or feet?
answers.intake_q7: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your back pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a7: I tried tylenol, but it didn't help very much
answers.intake_q8: Any shortness of breath or chest pain associated with your back pain?
answers.intake_q9: Any history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections?
answers.intake_q10: Have you noticed any swelling, bruising, or tenderness over the painful area?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that began approximately three weeks ago. She denies any recent trauma or injury, fever, chills, unexplained weight loss, numbness, tingling, weakness, shortness of breath, or chest pain. The pain improves with better posture and is associated with tenderness over the affected area. She has tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) with minimal relief. There is no history of osteoporosis, cancer, or recent infections. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain likely related to postural strain or soft tissue irritation localized to the left lower rib cage area. 

Differential: 
1. Musculoskeletal strain or myofascial pain 
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction 
3. Intercostal nerve irritation 
4. Early presentation of thoracic spine pathology (e.g., disc or facet joint) 

P: 
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid NSAIDs if history of renal or liver disease, gastrointestinal bleeding, or allergy; acetaminophen should not exceed 3,000 mg daily to prevent hepatotoxicity
 - Alternatives: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen if no contraindications 
 - Acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hours as needed for pain (use caution in liver disease; consider lower max daily dose)
 - Return precautions: Return promptly if pain worsens, new neurological symptoms develop (numbness, weakness), fever or chills occur, or if there is new chest pain or shortness of breath 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time unless symptoms worsen or new concerning features arise

evidence.title: Back pain - NHS
evidence.snippet: Find out how to relieve back pain, what can cause it, and when to get medical advice.

evidence.title: Back Pain
evidence.snippet: Lumbago is pain on the lower part of your back. Back pain is very common. Treatment varies from medicines to surgery depending on the cause and kind of pain.
